params.text:
The law behind moving and handling

Several pieces of UK legislation shape how care providers must manage moving and handling:

  • Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 (HASAWA): the foundation. Employers must protect the health, safety and welfare of staff and anyone affected by their work, so far as is reasonably practicable.
  • Manual Handling Operations Regulations 1992 (MHOR): deals specifically with lifting, lowering, pushing, pulling, carrying and moving loads — including people.
  • Lifting Operations and Lifting Equipment Regulations 1998 (LOLER): governs equipment such as hoists and slings that lift people.

The Care Quality Commission (CQC) inspects services against these duties. Providers must show that staff are trained, that risks are assessed, and that people are moved safely and with dignity.

Everyone has duties

The law shares responsibility between the employer and you:

  • Employers must avoid hazardous handling where they can, carry out risk assessments, provide suitable equipment and maintain it, and give staff proper training and refreshers.
  • Employees must follow the training and safe systems of work provided, use equipment correctly, take reasonable care for their own safety and that of others, and report risks, concerns or faulty equipment.

Health and safety is a shared duty — ignoring your employer's procedures puts you and the people you support at risk, and can be a disciplinary matter.

params.question: Which set of regulations deals specifically with lifting, lowering, carrying and moving loads, including people?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. MHOR is the main law dealing with hazardous manual handling of loads, including moving people.
No — that law is about personal information, not manual handling.
No — that concerns food hygiene and safety, not handling loads.
No — that governs decision-making for people who may lack capacity, not manual handling.
